purpose: Narrative hook that opens the whole page. 3–4 sentences. Establishes the theme, gives one surprising fact, and ends with a consequence or open question that makes the reader want to know more.

bands.6-8: Short sentences. One concrete image a child can picture. End with something that feels personal — 'that means you've never seen...' or 'scientists don't know yet'.
bands.9-12: Introduce one statistic or surprising number. Make the stakes feel real. A tension between what we know and what we don't is good.
bands.13-16: Full nuance. If there's an ethical or systemic dimension, surface it. Don't hand-hold. Trust the reader.

purpose: A real person connected to the theme. 2–3 punchy facts that make them memorable, not a biography. End with a quote if there's a great one.

bands.6-8: Lead with the most jaw-dropping thing they did. One sentence each. No titles or jargon.
bands.9-12: Include a role or credential, then the achievement, then why it matters now.
bands.13-16: Focus on what they changed, who they challenged, and what's still unresolved.

purpose: Exactly 3 multiple-choice questions. One easy, one medium, one hard. Format: question on its own line, then 4 options separated by ' · '. No answers shown.

bands.6-8: Questions based on the story content — answers should be discoverable from reading it. No trick questions.
bands.9-12: Mix of recall and inference. One question should require a small logical leap.
bands.13-16: At least one question that a thoughtful adult might get wrong. Can include contested or nuanced answers.

purpose: 1–2 book or article recommendations directly linked to the theme. For each: title, author, one sentence on why it's worth reading. Lean toward narrative non-fiction or literary fiction — not textbooks.

bands.6-8: Picture books or early chapter books only. Must be widely available.
bands.9-12: Middle-grade or accessible adult non-fiction. Avoid anything dated or dry.
bands.13-16: Young adult or adult literary. Can include challenging or morally complex texts.

purpose: One open discussion question for the dinner table or classroom. Must be genuinely debatable — no obvious right answer. Should feel urgent, not academic.

bands.6-8: Concrete and personal. 'Would you...', 'What would you do if...'. Avoid abstract concepts.
bands.9-12: Can involve fairness, tradeoffs, or competing interests. Still grounded in the concrete.
bands.13-16: Systemic, ethical, or political dimensions welcome. Should be provocative without being leading.

purpose: A genuine dilemma — both options should be appealing and unappealing in different ways, so there's no obvious right answer. Connected to the theme. Format: Option A description, then '— or —', then Option B.

bands.6-8: Sensory and concrete. 'Live underwater vs live in the clouds.' Funny is good.
bands.9-12: Involve real tradeoffs — isolation vs danger, comfort vs adventure.
bands.13-16: Can involve ethical tradeoffs, not just personal preference.

purpose: One genuinely surprising scientific fact or mechanism related to the theme. 2–3 sentences. Must be weird enough to make someone say 'wait, really?'

bands.6-8: Use an analogy to something familiar. No technical vocabulary without an immediate explanation.
bands.9-12: Name the mechanism or process. One technical term is fine if explained.
bands.13-16: Can go deeper into the mechanism. Evolutionary, chemical, or physical explanations welcome.

purpose: A philosophical or ethical question that has no easy answer and connects to the theme. 1–2 sentences max. The question itself is the content — don't try to answer it.

bands.6-8: Imaginative and empathetic. 'If animals could vote...' or 'What if we couldn't...'
bands.9-12: Fairness, rights, or responsibility. Still approachable but genuinely open.
bands.13-16: Philosophical, ethical, or political. Should be uncomfortable in a productive way.

purpose: A real historical event connected to the theme. Lead with the date (e.g. '23 Jan 1960'). Then 2 sentences: what happened, and why it still matters or what it changed.

bands.6-8: Focus on the human story — who was there, what they saw. One clear consequence.
bands.9-12: Include the historical context. Why was this moment possible at this point in history?
bands.13-16: What did this event challenge or prove? Include any controversy or ongoing debate.

purpose: A specific real place connected to the theme. 2–3 striking facts — size, depth, population, record, danger. Make it feel real and specific, not like a geography textbook entry.

bands.6-8: Use comparisons to things kids know — 'as tall as 10 double-decker buses stacked up'. One wow-fact.
bands.9-12: Include location, one record or superlative, and one human element.
bands.13-16: Can include political, environmental, or cultural complexity about the place.

purpose: One vocabulary word connected to the theme. Pronunciation in IPA. Etymology (where the word comes from). One sentence of definition. Optionally: how to use it in a sentence.

bands.6-8: Simple word with a surprising origin. Focus on the story of where it came from.
bands.9-12: Can be a more technical term. Etymology + how it connects to the theme.
bands.13-16: Can be a genuinely difficult word. Explore nuance of meaning or contested usage.

purpose: One striking statistic or fact. 1–2 sentences. Should be the kind of thing people repeat at dinner. Must be verifiable — include enough specificity to be fact-checkable.

bands.6-8: Relatable scale. Avoid percentages — use 'out of every 10' or comparisons.
bands.9-12: Statistics are fine. Give enough context that the number is meaningful.
bands.13-16: Can include methodological nuance — 'as of 2023', 'according to X study'. Readers can handle uncertainty.

purpose: One common misconception related to the theme, corrected. Format: 'myth statement' labelled Myth, then the corrected reality. 2 sentences total — one for the myth, one for the fact.

bands.6-8: Choose myths kids themselves commonly believe. Keep correction concrete and visual.
bands.9-12: Can be myths adults hold too. The correction should be genuinely surprising.
bands.13-16: Can challenge media narratives or common cultural assumptions. More nuanced corrections welcome.

purpose: A specific, achievable activity for tonight or this weekend. Must be concrete — not 'learn more about' but 'find out', 'try to', 'ask someone'. Related to the theme.

bands.6-8: Something physical or social — show someone, find something, count something.
bands.9-12: Can involve light research, a conversation, or a creative task. Should take <30 mins.
bands.13-16: Can be a deeper investigation, a position paper in their head, or a conversation with a specific type of person.

purpose: An open creative prompt — draw, write, design, imagine something related to the theme. 1–2 sentences. Should be genuinely open-ended with no right answer.

bands.6-8: Drawing or simple writing. Very concrete starting point — 'draw a creature that...'
bands.9-12: Writing, design, or invention. Give one constraint to spark ideas.
bands.13-16: Can be a written argument, creative fiction, or a design brief. Some intellectual stretch expected.
